item.title: Gedenkstein Melitta Bentz in Dresden, Germany
item.description: Melitta Bentz liked to drink coffee. But at the beginning of the 20th century, coffee was not prepared as it is today. When enjoying coffee, the coffee grounds in her mouth and the somewhat bitter aft

item.title: Sir Alex Ferguson pays tribute to former rival Kevin Keegan
item.description: Former Manchester United manager Sir Alex Ferguson says Kevin Keegan "carried the love of the game on his sleeve" as he paid tribute to his former rival.

item.title: King cobras may be riding trains across India [COMIC]
item.description: The snakes may be tempted onboard by tasty rodents — but train rides can carry cobras far from their forest habitats.

item.title: GB's Josh Kerr breaks mile WORLD RECORD
item.description: The Scotsman broke a world record that had been standing for 27 years.

item.title: Chatty cats and talking dogs could one day ‘speak’ in our language
item.description: Scientists are making advances in decoding animal sounds. That might someday make animal translators a possibility.

item.title: The mystery of the ‘Plain of Jars’ in Laos may now be solved
item.description: The remains of at least 37 people sit in an ancient stone “jar.” They suggest thousands of such jars in Laos are burial vaults.

item.title: Big waves shape this surfer-scientist’s research and life
item.description: Professor and pro surfer Cliff Kapono studies human-ocean interactions through the lens of a surfer, from molecular to macro scales.

item.title: Physics explains why gold doesn’t ‘rust’
item.description: Metals like copper tarnish when they react with oxygen in the air. Gold doesn’t, thanks to a quick rearrangement of atoms on its surface.

item.title: Fuzzy microbes may fuel cows’ methane burps
item.description: Production of this potent greenhouse gas is triggered by a newfound feature of these microbes — one with a funny name: hydrogenobody.

item.title: Trying to break a photon would create a quantum mess
item.description: Attempting to cut a light particle in half would spawn up to infinite new photons, a new model shows.

item.title: Drops of Rain in Étretat, France
item.description: Situated within the Jardin Emotions of the Jardins d'Étretat, one can find the "Drops of Rain" sculptures by Samuel Salcedo, that pout, grin, and stick their tongues out at visitors. These sculptures

item.title: Here’s how ‘singing’ mice belt out their tiny tunes
item.description: Balloon-like air sacs in the mice's throats create whistling songs. No other animal is known to make calls in this way.

item.title: Swissair Flight 111 Memorial in Peggys Cove Preservation Area, Nova Scotia
item.description: On September 2, 1998 Swissair Flight 111 crashed into the Atlantic Ocean. All 215 passengers and 14 crew members perished in the crash. The crash site is near entrance to St. Margaret’s Bay, approx. 8

item.title: Teddy Bear Park in Stillwater, Minnesota
item.description: Beloved toys around the world, teddy bears are icons of childhood wonder. These plush effigies put soft paws and friendly faces on one of nature's most dangerous creatures. Normally, they're tiny, con

item.title: Muse of Vistalegre Mural in Madrid, Spain
item.description: In the southern Madrid neighborhood of Carabanchel, a monumental mural has been unveiled, now holding the title of the largest in Spain. The Palacio de Vistalegre Arena, originally conceived as a bull

item.title: Museo Casa del Mendrugo in Puebla, Mexico
item.description: La Casa del Mendrugo occupies an old colonial mansion in Puebla. Its name, Mendrugo, refers to the crumbs or charity that the Jesuits received from the parishioners in order to support their ministry.

item.title: Umtanum Creek Footbridge in Ellensburg, Washington
item.description: Yakima (pronounced "YAK-uh-maw") Canyon, where the Yakima River traverses a water gap through Manastash Ridge, is an obvious thoroughfare and was used by Native Americans for thousands of years, and t

item.title: Cave Rock in Glenbrook, Nevada
item.description: Cave Rock, which sits on the southeastern side of Lake Tahoe, is a 360-foot-high and 800-foot-wide geological marvel that archaeologists say was formed by a five-million-year-old volcano vent. Thousan

item.title: British Runner ‘Calls His Shot’ and Breaks World Record in the Mile–After it Stood for 27 Years
item.description: Scottish runner Josh Kerr just put in the fastest mile time in human history, breaking a record that had stood for 27 years after clocking 3:42.66. Set at the recent London Diamond League athletics me

item.title: Easton’s Crayola Crayons and Markers Statues in Easton, Pennsylvania
item.description: These colorful statues are on display in downtown Easton as a reminder of the birthplace of Crayola. Around 1885 Edwin Binney and C. Harold Smith set out on a mission to expand their business. They wa
